what_it_does: Implements the Workshop canvas — the visual editor for composing real deliverables (slide decks and one-page brochures live; widget and wizard route to their own builders; website/social/email/video are coming-soon). It manages a list view and an edit view over 'docs' (pages → sections/blocks), with full editing operations (add/remove/move/duplicate/lock pages and sections, drag-and-drop reorder, layout picking, theme/motif selection), an undo/redo history, keyboard shortcuts, a candidate gallery driven by streaming Vi generation, per-field Vi regeneration, per-section refine and variation generation, AI slide-fill, a save-as-template flow with Vi-extracted variables, and a workspace-variables rail with {{key}} reference counting and a Vi audit. Most blocks render through the one shared engine (window.__cvRenderType). Exposes Workshop and WS_viDraft on window.
observations: DOC_TYPES lists 8 doc types with active flags: deck/brochure/widget/wizard active; website/social/email/video coming-soon.
useExtraDocTypes pulls user/vi-authored layer='doc' Types from CV_REGISTRY into a 'From your Type Registry' group on the picker — registry-driven doc types.
Large stateful component (~30 useState hooks aliased as useState_w) covering view/doc/draft/gallery/template/theme/library state.
Bridges to window.WS_AI.bridge.setActive so a chat rail / cross-doc surfaces can act on the active doc; clears on unmount.
Undo/redo via JSON snapshots (last 30) with an ignoreNextChange ref to avoid re-snapshotting on undo/redo.
Keyboard handler: Cmd+Z/Cmd+Shift+Z/Cmd+Y undo/redo, Cmd+D duplicate slide (deck), arrows for page nav, Escape to deselect.
runGallery drives window.WS_AI.generateCandidatesStream with per-candidate streaming callbacks; pickCandidate applies a diff via WS_AI.applyDiff/invertDiff.
Per-field regen wires window.__cvFieldFocus and WS_FIELD.setRegenHandler with angle-based titles (shorter/formal/specific/different).
refineSection/generateVariations/fillSlideWithVi build prompts that all inject window.CV_AI.get('voice.conceptv').text — voice sourced from the AI registry, not inlined.
viDraft generates a deck/brochure via CV_AI.execute('deck.generate', ...), with a parseJsonLoose recovery helper and normalization against WS_BLOCKS defaults and WS_KINDS.
saveAsTemplate calls WS_AI.extractTemplate to find variables, with a fallback to a plain copy; confirmSaveTemplate writes a workshop-doc template via saveTemplate.
VariablesRail: workspace vars with live reference counts (WS_VARS.countReferences), a Vi 'find' audit proposing candidate variables, color-aware inputs, and {{key}} usage.

Blocks render through window.__cvRenderType except embedWidget/embedWizard (WS_ENGINE_EDIT_SKIP) which keep their own renderer; wsSetPath is a dotted-path immutable setter for engine onEdit writeback.
SlideFrame fits a 1280-wide slide to the container via ResizeObserver scaling and renders slide chrome (hatch edge, page number, brand mark) with inline SVG.
ViSuggestionsRail shows instant heuristic suggestions then enriches asynchronously via WS_AI.viSuggest, with dismiss + refresh.
standouts: One shared rendering engine (__cvRenderType) renders AND edits nearly every block kind via a runtime descriptor, with only cross-doc embeds excepted — strong single-engine discipline.
Voice is consistently pulled from CV_AI.get('voice.conceptv').text in every generation prompt rather than inlined — adheres to the project's no-second-home rule for voice.
Workspace variables form a live single-source system: define {{key}} once, reference anywhere, with live cross-doc reference counts and a Vi audit to extract repeated literals into variables.
Candidate generation is streamed (per-candidate callbacks update the gallery incrementally) and diffs are invertible (invertDiff reserved for a Vi-undo stack).
Registry-authored doc Types appear in the picker alongside built-ins, mapping to a built-in family as a rendering fallback.
conventions: Registry/AI-as-truth: doc types extend from CV_REGISTRY, generation routes through CV_AI.execute/complete, voice from the CV_AI behaviour — consistent with the project charter.
Single-engine rendering via __cvRenderType with a small explicit skip-set, rather than per-block bespoke renderers.
window-global component/helper pattern; exports Workshop and WS_viDraft on window; aliased React hooks.
parseJsonLoose recovery pattern for all model JSON responses.
Heavy inline styling that references design tokens via var(); loud-fail-to-toast on AI failures.
Immutable update helpers throughout (map/filter/splice on copies, wsSetPath).
concerns: Very large multi-responsibility file (~1900 lines) holding the main component plus ~15 sub-components and several module helpers — high cognitive load and many cross-component prop chains.
Pervasive inline style objects duplicate styling that could be CSS classes (tokenised values, but layout/typography hand-set per element).
Many optional globals chained with ?. (WS_AI, WS_VARS, WS_BLOCKS, WidgetBuilder, TypeThumb, etc.); some paths assume presence (e.g. window.WS_BLOCKS[kind] in addSection without a guard).
Native confirm() used for destructive actions (remove page/section/doc, remove variable).
viDraft references module-scope `doc` (doc?.type) but `doc` is a component-local — at module scope `doc` is undefined, so the surface arg falls back; a subtle scoping oddity.
Variations/audit/preview items frequently keyed by array index; some onError callbacks 'swallow per-candidate errors' silently.
notes: The Workshop is the deliverable composer: pages of blocks rendered/edited through the one engine, with deep Vi assistance (draft, refine, variations, slide-fill, candidate gallery, template extraction) and a live {{var}} workspace system; widget/wizard docs delegate to their own builders.
questions: What is the full WS_AI surface (bridge, generateCandidatesStream, applyDiff/invertDiff, extractTemplate, viSuggest, materializeTemplate, computeSuggestions)?
How does WS_VARS.countReferences scan docs, and how are {{key}} references resolved at render time?
Why does module-scope viDraft reference `doc` (a component local) — is that intentional or a latent bug?
Where are WS_BLOCKS / WS_LAYOUTS / WS_THEMES / WS_MOTIFS / WS_KINDS defined and how do they relate to the Type Registry?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the header comment (visual canvas for composing real deliverables; deck+brochure live, others coming) accurately describes the file, and the coming-soon flags are honestly reflected in the picker.
apparent_themes: One shared generative engine renders and edits everything.
Voice single-sourced from the AI registry.
Deep but optional Vi assistance layered over direct manual editing.
intention_signals: Aims to be the place where real, on-brand deliverables are composed visually — every block flowing through the one engine, every value definable once as a variable, and Vi available at every grain (whole-doc transform, slide fill, section refine/variations, per-field alternates, template extraction) while keeping manual control and undo safety.
novelty: The combination of single-engine render-and-edit, a live cross-doc {{var}} reference-counting system with a Vi extraction audit, streaming invertible candidate galleries, and registry-authored doc types appearing beside built-ins — all in one composition surface.
contribution: Contributes the full Workshop deliverable-composition surface: the page/section/block editing model and operations, undo/redo + keyboard layer, the streaming candidate gallery and per-field/per-section/whole-doc Vi flows, the save-as-template-with-extracted-variables modal, the workspace-variables rail, and the single-engine block rendering via __cvRenderType.
summary_for_embedding: A large React canvas implementing the Workshop deliverable editor (decks/brochures) with page/block editing, undo/redo, a streaming Vi candidate gallery, per-section/per-field/whole-doc AI, template extraction, and a live {{var}} workspace, rendering blocks through one shared engine.
intention_for_embedding: To let users visually compose real on-brand deliverables where every block renders through one engine, every value is a reusable variable, and Vi assists at every grain while preserving manual control and undo.
